matches.match_rationale: The core problem is that critical IT knowledge (SIS batch job resets, LMS workarounds, database restore procedures) is trapped in a few individuals' heads. RAG directly addresses this by ingesting whatever documentation exists — runbooks, email threads, chat logs, incident tickets — and making it queryable. Staff can ask 'how do I reset the SIS batch job?' and get a grounded, cited answer rather than calling Maria. This is technically feasible with existing LLM APIs and a modest document corpus, requiring no custom model training.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An internal IT knowledge base chatbot that ingests existing documentation, email archives, and incident logs from SIS/LMS/finance systems. New hires and junior IT staff query it for step-by-step guidance on undocumented procedures, reducing bus factor risk immediately.

matches.match_rationale: A significant part of the problem is sparse, outdated documentation. AI-assisted text generation can accelerate knowledge capture by prompting senior IT staff with structured templates and auto-drafting runbooks, SOPs, and restore procedures from their verbal or written inputs. This lowers the activation energy for documentation — instead of writing from scratch, experts review and correct AI-generated drafts. Feasible immediately using off-the-shelf LLM APIs with minimal setup.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A documentation co-pilot that interviews IT staff via structured prompts and auto-generates runbooks, troubleshooting guides, and system procedure docs for SIS, LMS, and finance systems — turning tribal knowledge into written assets before key staff leave.

matches.match_rationale: Multi-site education operators accumulate knowledge in fragmented forms — long email threads about outages, sprawling IT tickets, recorded troubleshooting calls, and legacy wiki pages. Summarization can condense these into concise, actionable runbooks without requiring senior staff to manually rewrite them. This directly addresses the 'documentation sparse or outdated' dimension of the pain point by recycling existing artifacts into usable knowledge.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated pipeline that ingests historical IT support tickets, incident email chains, and system logs, and outputs condensed troubleshooting guides per system (SIS, LMS, finance) — creating a living knowledge base from existing communication artifacts.

matches.match_rationale: Several of the described pain points are procedural: SIS batch job resets, database backup verification, restore procedure execution. These are multi-step workflows where the knowledge of what to do is the bottleneck. Task automation agents can encode these procedures as executable workflows — replacing the 'call Maria' dependency with an automated agent that handles the steps autonomously. This directly reduces bus factor risk for routine operational procedures.
matches.opportunity_sketch: Automated IT operation agents that encode known procedures (SIS batch resets, backup verification, routine LMS maintenance) as agentic workflows — replacing individual expert dependency with documented, executable automation that any IT staff member can trigger.

matches.match_rationale: New hires spending months learning undocumented workarounds is a core cost of this pain point. A conversational IT assistant that maintains dialogue state — walking a new hire through a multi-step SIS issue, asking clarifying questions, and providing contextual guidance — directly addresses the onboarding and first-response dimensions. Unlike RAG (single-turn Q&A), a conversational agent can guide someone through an unfamiliar procedure interactively, approximating the experience of asking Maria.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An interactive IT helpdesk assistant that guides staff through system issues conversationally — asking diagnostic questions, stepping through resolution procedures, and escalating only when genuinely novel. Reduces dependency on senior staff for routine troubleshooting.
